text.title: Risk Threshold Policy — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every risk threshold policy must satisfy. The rules exist so the policy can be read at 11pm and produce the right routing decision without judgement calls.

rule.statement: The policy MUST partition the severity scale into 3-5 explicit bands (e.g. green ≤6, amber 7-15, red 16-25). Continuous-score "use judgement" routing is forbidden.

rationale.source: PMBOK 7 risk governance; APM Body of Knowledge

rule.rationale: Bands convert continuous scores into discrete escalation decisions. A PM looking at "score 14" should see "amber band → notify sponsor by EOD Friday" without re-evaluating. The bands are the policy's primary user interface.

rule.statement: Each band routes to a NAMED individual (role + person), not a group inbox or committee. "Notify the steering committee" without a named chair is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Named targets are accountable; groups diffuse. The named target may delegate to a group, but the policy's contract is with the person. If the named person is unavailable, the policy specifies a fallback (deputy) by name.

rule.statement: Each band has an explicit SLA: a maximum time from "PM identifies the risk crossed the threshold" to "named target receives the escalation". Red ≤2h, amber ≤24h, green = weekly digest. Bands without SLA are forbidden.

rationale.source: Incident-management best practice; APM escalation guidance

rule.rationale: SLAs convert "I'll escalate when I get to it" into a binding promise. The SLA is what makes the policy a contract; sponsors who agree to be the named target for red also agree to be reachable within the SLA.

rule.statement: Each band defines explicit de-escalation criteria: what must change for the risk to move down. Without criteria, risks ratchet upward and never close.

rationale.source: ITIL incident lifecycle; project-management postmortem analysis

rule.rationale: A risk register without de-escalation rules becomes a museum of past worries. The criteria force the PM to define what good looks like — mitigations applied, probability reduced, sponsor briefed, etc. — and the named target signs off on the descent.

rule.statement: The policy is co-signed by sponsor + delivery lead + PMO (or equivalent governance) at engagement kickoff. Refresh required at every milestone (e.g. phase gate) OR every 12 weeks, whichever first.

rationale.source: PMI standards; field experience with mid-project escalation disputes

rule.rationale: When a red risk arrives, the sponsor must not be able to claim they did not know they were the escalation target. Co-signature pre-commits the relationship. Periodic refresh keeps the policy in sync with team changes.

section.title: Canonical band table
section.code: green (1-6): PM logs in risk register; weekly digest to delivery lead; no real-time notification amber (7-15): PM pings delivery lead within 24h; delivery lead briefs sponsor at next standup red (16-20): PM emails sponsor within 4h with mitigation proposal; sponsor acknowledges within 24h black (21-25): PM phone calls sponsor within 2h; emergency steering call within 24h; PMO informed

example.title: Policy in action
example.code: Tuesday 14:30 — PM scores newly-discovered vendor-delay risk at 4×4=16 (red). Tuesday 14:35 — PM pulls policy: red → email sponsor within 4h with mitigation proposal. Tuesday 16:10 — Email sent with: risk, mitigation A/B, recommendation, decision deadline Friday. Tuesday 17:42 — Sponsor acknowledges within SLA. Friday 11:00 — Decision: option B. Risk re-scored at 3×3=9 (amber). De-escalation criteria met (signed contract with backup vendor). Risk closed at next standup.
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5. PM did not invent the routing; the policy did.

example.title: No policy, all-or-nothing escalation
example.code: PM identifies red risk Monday morning. PM hesitates because "the sponsor is in a board meeting all day". PM raises in steering committee Thursday. Sponsor: "Why didn't you tell me sooner?" — relationship damaged.
example.note: r3 violation (no SLA), r2 violation (no named target with reachability commitment). The fault is structural, not personal.
